title: Feasibility of Wrist-Worn, Cancelable, Real-Time Biometric Authentication via HD-sEMG and Dynamic Gestures
venue: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement

method.input_representation: sEMG feature images (2x10 layout based on Mean Absolute Value)
method.training_strategy: Cross-entropy loss with additive voting of gesture distribution probabilities across sliding windows

evaluation.context: Achieved in Situation 2 (imposters performing correct gesture code) with a code length of 12
Achieved in Situation 3 (cancelability test where imposters use user's biometric traits)
Real-time authentication test with a single user vs 23 imposters
Real-time authentication test with a single user vs 23 imposters

evaluation_protocol: Three situations: Situation 1 (imposters unknown code), Situation 2 (imposters known code), and Situation 3 (cancelability/biometric trait leakage). User-specific models trained on individual data.

reported_limitations: Performance degradation due to electrode shifts between days
Influence of different arm postures on sEMG signals

usability.usability_issues: Long code lengths required for between-day stability hinder fast access

key_contribution: Verified feasibility of 16-channel wrist-worn HD-sEMG for biometric authentication
Implementation of cancelable authentication via dynamic gesture codes
main_strength: High accuracy (EER < 0.01) with significantly fewer sensors than previous forearm-based systems
Faster authentication than static gesture methods
main_weakness: Significant performance drop in between-day tests
Testing limited to a single arm posture (elbow supported on table)
insights.notes_for_review: The system demonstrates a strong trade-off between security (code length) and speed. Between-day degradation is a primary bottleneck.
